The the unusual thing that does at that point is it separates out the header from the response from the payload of the response. And the header goes up back over uniccast to the edge proxy. That's the thing that sits in the residential gateway and does the conversion from uniccast back to from multiccast back to uniccast. So the the head comes back to that. The payload is put on multiccast that also goes back to that edge proxy. they're combined and the full response is then returned to the player and the player is none the wiser that anything has happened. So so far this is not very interesting. We find a really complicated way of doing exactly what we do currently. It gets more interesting with the second and subsequent requests for the same video segment in which case the root proxy actually converts them into a head request. So the CDN doesn't need to return the payload but then that that head request which is possibly unique to that uh that uh particular request comes back from the CDN to the root proxy and is just passed over uniccast to the the edge proxy that that request came through that's already had the payload by multiccast and so the two are combined and returned to the player. What this means is that all requests are seen by the CDN. So they know how many people are requesting particular segments, how many clients are and they have a degree of control.
